ai_answer:
**Who is Ajit Pawar?**

Ajit Pawar is an Indian politician and senior leader of the Nationalist Congress Party (NCP), a key political party in the Indian state of Maharashtra. He has been a prominent figure in Maharashtra's politics for over four decades, playing a crucial role in the state's governance.

**Early Life and Education**

Ajit Pawar was born on February 22, 1957, in Baramati, a small town in Pune district, Maharashtra. He studied at the Government College in Pune and graduated in Bachelor's degree of Commerce. After completing his education, Pawar joined a small business in the family. However, he soon became involved in politics and joined the NCP's precursor, the Indian National Congress.

**Rise to Power**

Pawar rose through the ranks of the NCP, becoming a close associate of the party's founder, Sharad Pawar. He served as a Member of the Legislative Assembly (MLA) from Baramati constituency in 1991. Over the years, he became increasingly influential within the party, and in 1999, he was made the NCP's Maharashtra unit's working president.

**Positions Held**

Ajit Pawar has held several important positions in the NCP and the Maharashtra government:

1.  **Leader of the Opposition in the Maharashtra Legislative Assembly (1999-2009):** Pawar held this position in the Maharashtra Assembly, leading the NCP's opposition to the government led by the Shiv Sena-Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) alliance.
2.  **Minister of Water Resources (2009-2012):** In 2009, Pawar was made the Minister of Water Resources in the Maharashtra government, led by Prithviraj Chavan. He played a key role in implementing various water management schemes in the state.
3. **Deputy Chief Minister of Maharashtra (2012-2014 and 2019):** In 2012, Pawar became the Deputy Chief Minister of Maharashtra, serving under Prithviraj Chavan. In 2019, he again took on this position, under Devendra Fadnavis (BJP) and later took oath as the Deputy CM in the Uddhav Thackeray-led government.
4. **Minister of Revenue and Finance (2012-2014):** As the Minister of Revenue and Finance, Pawar played a crucial role in formulating and implementing financial policies for the state.

**Corruption Allegations and Controversies**

Ajit Pawar has been associated with several controversies and corruption allegations throughout his career:

1. **Water Scam (2012):** A CAG report revealed that the Maharashtra government had allocated Rs. 10.2 billion for the irrigation projects, but only a small fraction of the allocated funds were actually used. Pawar was among those accused of misusing funds meant for irrigation projects.
2. **Land Scam (2014):** Pawar faced allegations of land scams, with accusations that he and his family members had acquired land meant for government projects at throwaway prices.

**Electoral Record**

Ajit Pawar has a consistent electoral track record as an MLA. He won his seat unopposed in 2014 and won by a margin of over 90,000 votes in 2019.

**Awards and Recognition**

In 2013, Pawar was awarded the Maharashtra Bhushan award for his contributions to politics and public service.

**References:**

1. [Wikipedia - Ajit Pawar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ajit_Pawar)
2. [News18 - Ajit Pawar's Biography](https://www.news18.com/newspixels/india/ajit-pawar-profile-biography-baramati-mla-maharashtra-nationalist-congress-party-ncp-2478138.html)
3. [India Today - Ajit Pawar's Electoral Record](https://www.indiatoday.in/india/story/ajit-pawar-election-results-baramati-assembly-constituency-maharashtra-1711244-2020-10-21)
4. [DNA India - Ajit Pawar's Scandals](https://www.dnaindia.com/india/report-scandals-that-rocked-n-c-p-s-supremo-sharad-pawar-son-ajit-pawar-s-career-2791231)

sections.content_snippet: See also: [Political families of Maharashtra](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Political_families_of_Maharashtra "Political families of Maharashtra") Pawar's grandparents were Govindrao and Sharda Pawar.[\[6\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ajit_Pawar#cite_note-:1-7)[\[7\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ajit_Pawar#cite_note-:3-8) Govindrao was associated with [worker cooperatives](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Worker_cooperative "Worker cooperative") in [Baramati](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Baramati "Baramati"), while Sharda managed the family farm.[\[6\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ajit_Pawar#cite_note-:1-7) In 1938, she was elected to the Pune local board.[\[7\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ajit_Pawar#cite_note-:3-8) The couple had eleven children, including seven sons and four daughters;[\[7\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ajit_Pawar#cite_note-:3-8) one of their sons, named
sections.heading: Ancestry

sections.content_snippet: Ajit Anantrao Pawar[\[8\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ajit_Pawar#cite_note-9) was born on 22 July 1959 in [Deolali Pravara](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deolali_Pravara "Deolali Pravara"), [Maharashtra](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maharashtra "Maharashtra"),[\[9\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ajit_Pawar#cite_note-:2-10)[\[10\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ajit_Pawar#cite_note-11) to Anantrao and Ashatai Pawar.[\[7\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ajit_Pawar#cite_note-:3-8) His father died when he was eighteen years old.[\[9\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ajit_Pawar#cite_note-:2-10) He had a brother named Shriniwas and a sister named Vijaya.[\[6\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ajit_Pawar#cite_note-:1-7) In 1985,[\[11\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ajit_Pawar#cite_note-12) he married [Sunetra Pawar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sunetra_Pawar "Sunetra Pawar") (née Patil), the daughter of [Padamsinh Bajirao Patil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Padamsinh_Bajirao_Patil "Padamsinh Bajirao Patil"),
sections.heading: Early life

sections.content_snippet: Pawar was elected to the [Lok Sabha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lok_Sabha "Lok Sabha") for the first time in 1991 from the [Baramati Parliamentary constituency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Baramati_Lok_Sabha_constituency "Baramati Lok Sabha constituency"). He later vacated the seat for his uncle, who then became the [Defence Minister](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minister_of_Defence_(India) "Minister of Defence (India)") in Prime Minister [P. V. Narasimha Rao](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/P._V._Narasimha_Rao "P. V. Narasimha Rao")'s government.[\[14\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ajit_Pawar#cite_note-15) Pawar was known for his blunt
sections.heading: Political career

sections.content_snippet: Main article: [2023 Maharashtra political crisis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2023_Maharashtra_political_crisis "2023 Maharashtra political crisis") On 23 November 2019, he defected from NCP and joined a government led by the [Bharatiya Janata Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bharatiya_Janata_Party "Bharatiya Janata Party") and became the [Deputy Chief Minister](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deputy_Chief_Minister_of_Maharashtra "Deputy Chief Minister of Maharashtra").[\[28\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ajit_Pawar#cite_note-29)[\[29\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ajit_Pawar#cite_note-30) He submitted a paper with the signatures of NCP's MLAs to the Governor to prove the government's majority.
sections.heading: Rebellion against Sharad Pawar

sections.content_snippet: In 2023, having the support of the majority of the erstwhile NCP's MLAs, he also claimed the position of president of the NCP, as well as the party's name and its [electoral symbol](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electoral_symbol "Electoral symbol").[\[32\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ajit_Pawar#cite_note-33)[\[33\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ajit_Pawar#cite_note-:0-34) Pawar joined the ruling [Maha Yuti](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maha_Yuti "Maha Yuti") coalition and took the oath as deputy CM of the state on 2 July.
